title: Week 13 sizing recommendation table
acceptanceTarget: small business can justify 4 vCPU / 8 GB RAM / PostgreSQL / backup / monitoring as a starting point or provide adjustment reasons
assumptions: These are starting recommendations for self-hosted planning, not absolute performance guarantees.
The n8n official server sizing table is treated as an input, while the actual recommendation depends on users, workflows, executions, binary size, and execution duration.
PostgreSQL is preferred once workflows become business-critical, shared by multiple people, or require backup and restore discipline.
Binary-heavy workflows should not stay on default memory binary mode in production.
Queue mode requires Redis and workers, and should use PostgreSQL rather than SQLite.

capacityDrivers.name: frequency
capacityDrivers.effect: Raises execution count, database writes, log volume, and API calls.

capacityDrivers.name: concurrency
capacityDrivers.effect: Raises simultaneous memory, CPU, event loop, database connection, and external API pressure.

capacityDrivers.name: binary size
capacityDrivers.effect: Determines whether memory, filesystem, database, or external object storage becomes the bottleneck.

capacityDrivers.name: wait states
capacityDrivers.effect: Keeps paused execution state available for resume and can retain data longer than normal pruning windows.

capacityDrivers.name: AI calls
capacityDrivers.effect: Adds external model latency, large prompt and response payloads, retries, and rate-limit behavior.

capacityDrivers.name: browser automation
capacityDrivers.effect: Adds host or worker process CPU, memory, temp file, screenshot, and download pressure.

profiles.label: learning
profiles.database: SQLite acceptable for short-lived learning; export workflows and preserve the encryption key if anything matters.
profiles.backup: Manual export and local snapshot are enough for training data.
profiles.concurrencyModel: Manual executions or very low-frequency triggers.
profiles.binaryDataModel: Default mode acceptable only for tiny files; use filesystem when testing file workflows.
warningSigns: Manual execution slows when inspecting large data
upgradeTriggers: A workflow becomes important enough that data loss matters

profiles.label: personal
profiles.database: SQLite is acceptable for low-risk personal automations; PostgreSQL is recommended for important always-on workflows.
profiles.backup: Daily volume snapshot or database dump when automations matter.
profiles.monitoring: Basic uptime, logs, disk usage, and failed execution review.
profiles.concurrencyModel: Low-frequency schedules and small webhook bursts.
profiles.binaryDataModel: Filesystem mode for recurring file work; avoid default memory mode for large files.
warningSigns: Webhook responses slow during file handling
Failed executions increase after adding large payloads
upgradeTriggers: More than a few production webhooks

profiles.label: freelancer

startingSpec.summary: 2 vCPU / 4 GB RAM, or 4 vCPU / 8 GB RAM when client workloads are steady

profiles.database: PostgreSQL recommended once client workflows or credentials are stored.
profiles.backup: Daily PostgreSQL backup and monthly restore drill.
profiles.monitoring: HTTP status, CPU, memory, disk, database size, failed executions, and backup status.
profiles.concurrencyModel: Moderate schedules, client webhooks, and some overlapping executions.
profiles.binaryDataModel: Filesystem mode with persistent volume; database mode only when queue-mode sharing requires it.
warningSigns: Client webhook latency varies by workload
Database size grows from execution history
BinaryData folder grows without predictable retention
upgradeTriggers: Multiple clients rely on the same instance

profiles.label: small business

startingSpec.summary: 4 vCPU / 8 GB RAM / PostgreSQL / backup / monitoring

profiles.database: PostgreSQL required as the baseline for workflows, credentials, execution history, backup, restore, and migration.
profiles.backup: Daily automated PostgreSQL backup, retention policy, and monthly restore drill.
profiles.monitoring: CPU, memory, restart count, webhook latency, failed executions, PostgreSQL size, PostgreSQL connections, binary storage, and backup status.
profiles.concurrencyModel: Start with regular mode and a conservative N8N_CONCURRENCY_PRODUCTION_LIMIT, then move to queue mode if webhook bursts become normal.
profiles.binaryDataModel: Filesystem mode on persistent volume for ordinary files; database or S3 for queue-mode or multi-worker binary-heavy workflows.
warningSigns: Memory stays above 70 percent during business hours
Webhook p95 latency approaches external provider timeout
PostgreSQL backup duration increases week over week
Binary storage grows without pruning relief
upgradeTriggers: AI calls or browser automation run alongside normal webhook traffic
Database storage or connections approach configured limits
profiles.acceptanceConclusion: For a small business, 4 vCPU / 8 GB RAM / PostgreSQL / backup / monitoring is a reasonable starting point because it covers shared production workflows, durable state, restore needs, and enough memory headroom for moderate binary data without overcommitting to queue mode on day one.

profiles.label: agency

startingSpec.summary: 4 to 8 vCPU / 16 GB RAM or separate worker pool

profiles.database: Managed PostgreSQL with restore drill, connection planning, and execution data retention rules.
profiles.backup: Point-in-time recovery or snapshot policy, restore runbook, and environment-specific retention.
profiles.monitoring: Dashboards and alerts for workers, queue depth, database, binary storage, webhook latency, failed executions, and cost-sensitive logs.
profiles.concurrencyModel: Queue mode with Redis and separate workers once client traffic overlaps.
profiles.binaryDataModel: Database or S3 mode depending on queue-mode architecture; avoid tying workers to a single local filesystem.
warningSigns: Queue backlog grows during client campaigns
One client's binary workflows slow down other clients
Database connection count increases with each worker
upgradeTriggers: Need deploy windows and rollback discipline
Need separate worker pools for heavy workflow classes

profiles.label: AI-heavy

startingSpec.summary: 8 vCPU / 16 to 32 GB RAM with dedicated workers for long-running AI and browser automation

profiles.database: Managed PostgreSQL with Redis queue mode and retention tuned for large execution payloads.
profiles.backup: Database backup plus object storage lifecycle and restore validation.
profiles.monitoring: Model latency, model error rate, retry count, queue depth, worker memory, browser process duration, binary storage, and backup duration.
profiles.concurrencyModel: Queue mode with worker pools separated by workload class.
profiles.binaryDataModel: S3 or database mode; never rely on default memory mode for large document, media, or screenshot flows.
warningSigns: Worker memory spikes during document processing
Headless browser jobs leave temp files or long-running processes
upgradeTriggers: RAG or document processing becomes core workload
Browser automation is used in production
Large files are retained for audit or human review
